label: Topology
definition: Geometry stripped down to what survives continuous deformation, stretching and bending but never tearing or gluing, so that a coffee mug and a doughnut are the same shape. It formalizes nearness without distance, and in machine learning it is the language for the qualitative shape of data: the manifold hypothesis is a topological claim, and a classifier cannot separate topologically entangled classes without first disentangling them.

body.text: Topology is geometry stripped down to what survives continuous deformation. It studies the properties of a space that do not change when you stretch, bend, or twist it, so long as you never tear it or glue distinct points together. To a topologist a coffee mug and a doughnut are the same object, each having exactly one hole, while a sphere and a doughnut are genuinely different, because no continuous deformation can add or remove a hole. The device that makes this rigorous is the topological space, a set together with a family of open sets satisfying simple axioms; open sets encode nearness directly, with no numerical distance, which is what lets topology apply even where no sensible metric exists.

With nearness defined by open sets, the central concepts follow. A continuous map is one whose preimages of open sets are open, the abstract version of a map with no sudden jumps, and a homeomorphism is a continuous map with a continuous inverse, the precise statement that two spaces are topologically identical. The properties topology cares about are the invariants preserved by homeomorphism: connectedness, compactness, and the structure of holes in various dimensions. Algebraic topology makes the holes computable by attaching algebraic objects to a space, the homology and homotopy groups, turning geometric features into group-theory data a computer can manipulate, the fundamental group recording loops that cannot be contracted and higher homology recording higher-dimensional voids.

In machine learning, topology shows up wherever the shape of data matters more than its coordinates. The manifold hypothesis, that high-dimensional data concentrates near a low-dimensional manifold, is fundamentally topological: it asserts the data has a simple intrinsic shape hidden inside a complicated ambient space. Dimensionality reduction and manifold learning try to recover that shape and unfold it, which is sound only when they respect the data's topology, preserving which points are near which and how the pieces connect; a method that tears or wrongly glues the manifold produces a misleading latent space. Topology also supplies the very setting in which a manifold is defined, a space that looks locally like flat space, on which the smooth structure of differential geometry then sits.

Topological data analysis turns these ideas into a concrete toolkit. Persistent homology examines a data set at all scales at once, recording which holes and connected components appear and how long they persist as the cloud of points is thickened, and summarizes the result in a persistence diagram that is robust to noise and invariant to rotation and stretching, capturing global features like loops and voids that distance-based summaries miss entirely. Topology also clarifies why certain tasks are hard: a classifier built from continuous layers cannot change the topology of its input without help, so separating classes that are topologically entangled forces the representation to disentangle them first. Across these uses, topology is the language for the qualitative shape of data, the complement to the quantitative, distance-based view of geometry.

image.alt: Animation of a coffee mug smoothly deforming into a doughnut-shaped torus and back, with the mug's handle becoming the hole of the torus.
image.caption: Topology studies the properties of a shape that survive continuous stretching and bending, with no tearing or gluing. By that standard a coffee mug and a doughnut are the same object: each has exactly one hole, and the animation deforms one into the other continuously, the handle becoming the torus hole.
